sections.paragraph:
to adorn with a neck chain or collar. (From the idea of choking, or strangling, which is that of the kindred roots אָנַק, חַנַק, which see. Arab. عنق IV. to ornament a dog with a collar. عُنْقُ neck, Germ. Nacken, Upper-Germ. die Anke.) Once used figuratively, Psa 73:6, עֲנָקַתְמֹו גַאֲוָה “pride surrounds them like a neck chain,” i.e. clothes their neck; a stiff neck being used poetically as the seat of pride.

Hiphil הֶעֱנִיק prob. to lay on the neck (to be carried), Deu 15:14, used of a slave set at liberty: הַעֲנֵיק תַּעֲנִיק לֹו מִצֹּאנְךָ וְגו׳ “thou shalt lay upon him of thy flock,” etc, LXX., Vulg. dabis viaticum. Others apply to the word the signification of giving, so that it would properly be to adorn with a collar; hence with gifts. As to what I formerly compared, on the authority of Castell and Giggeius, “عنق followed by على to shew oneself easy, gentle,” it rests on a mistake of Giggeius in rendering the words of the Kamûs (ii. p. 1318, edit. Calcutt.).
